topic.what: Volatility 3 plugin to detect and analyze TLS callbacks in Windows process memory dumps
topic.how: Parses PE headers to locate IMAGE_TLS_DIRECTORY, extracts callback addresses, disassembles instructions using Capstone, and applies pattern matching with regex and YARA rules
topic.why_matters: TLS callbacks are legitimate Windows constructs that malware exploits for stealthy execution before main program entry point, evading traditional detection methods

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of malicious patterns detected in TLS callbacks
characteristics.vs_baseline: Extends existing Volatility plugins (callbacks/malfind) which do not analyze TLS callbacks
characteristics.evidence: Detects code injection via dynamic memory, control flow hijacking, stack string construction, API hashing, NOP sleds
characteristics.context: Based on real malware techniques from Ursnif and Nadnazz bot

characteristics.evidence: Both 32-bit and 64-bit parsing implemented with architecture-specific pointer handling
characteristics.context: Windows PE executables on both architectures supported

characteristics.evidence: Uses Capstone for disassembly, integrates with Volatility 3 framework
characteristics.context: Plugin received recognition in Volatility 3 Plugin Contest 2024

characteristics.evidence: --disasm-bytes flag allows customization of disassembly window
characteristics.context: Addresses limitation of fixed output length in existing tools

characteristics.evidence: Tested with VMware .vmem snapshots from Windows 10 system

novelty: Context-aware TLS callback analysis combining extraction with automated disassembly and heuristic-based suspicious behavior detection
Recursive disassembly following unknown call instructions to reveal hidden functionality
Custom regex pattern matching integrated with YARA rules for comprehensive TLS callback scanning
Configurable disassembly bytes via --disasm-bytes flag (addresses fixed-length limitation in malfind)
open_problems: Cross-platform support beyond Windows not addressed
Accuracy improvements needed for false positive reduction
Automated malware classification using ML not implemented
Scalability testing with larger memory dumps not performed
